next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Noreen woke to the sound of rain that was not rain.

It was ice gritting against the front window, a dry furious hiss, and the window answered with little clicks like teeth. The coal in the range had sunk to a red eye. Her feet were already swollen before she put them on the rug, the skin stretched shiny where her slippers rubbed. She sat up slowly because the headache from the clinic warning had come back behind her right eye, a steel pin turned one quarter at a time.

The first pain crossed her belly while she reached for the chamber pot. It did not rise and fall. It took hold low and deep and dragged her breath out of her like someone pulling wet linen through a wringer. She stayed bent over the mattress until it eased. Then she counted. Keane had told her to count anything she could trust: clock ticks, kettle whistles, the distance between pains. Numbers did not panic.

She got to six minutes. Then five. On the third pain she stood and warm water spilled down her calf, clear at first, then clouded pink when it hit her heel.

"Elsie," she said to the wall before she remembered a wall did not answer.

Arthur was in the spare room on the other side of the landing. She knocked once and he was up at once, boots in his hand, shirt half buttoned. He slept in a wool cap with a rent at the crown because the back room chimney drew wrong and dropped soot on the pillows.

"It's started," she said. "It's not practice. It's proper." 

Arthur did not ask whether she was sure. He looked at the water on her nightdress hem and nodded once. "I'll fetch Mrs Harrow and ring Ke...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...you can't get to the wall, Arthur hears a kettle whistle better than any man on this street. He'll come. Keane said no waiting, and I heard her."

Noreen looked at her, really looked, at the pale strain around her mouth and the stubborn set of her jaw and the curve of her own late belly beneath the coat. Elsie was carrying life too, and still she was offering minutes and muscle she did not have to spare.

"This could ruin you," Noreen said.

"Tom already ruined enough. Peter can try."

She said it without drama, as if naming weather.

The next bus groaned into view, windows fogged, steps slick. Elsie climbed first, turned, and took Noreen's elbow with both hands. Noreen felt the careful pressure and understood, with a clarity that hurt, that love had crossed into logistics. It had become coins in wax paper, symptom lists on mantelpieces, who held a door, who answered a knock, who spoke first when a man said no.

Inside, the conductor punched their tickets and moved on. Noreen held the rail and breathed through the ache under her ribs. Elsie sat beside her, watching the street blur by, jaw tight, already sorting what could be shifted in both houses before evening. It was not romance now. It was systems.

On Barrow Lane the walls were thin, the money thinner, and death could come quietly between one cup of tea and the next. Elsie knew that in her bones by the time the bus turned the corner. Whatever they had called this before, whatever private name had kept it tender and hidden, it had changed.

From today, every choice they made about each other was also a medical decision.
